title: What are Sessions?
description: Understand why sessions are needed for state management.

slides.title: HTTP is Stateless
slides.content:
# The Problem: No Memory

HTTP, the protocol of the web, is **stateless**. Each request from a browser to your server is independent. The server doesn't know that two requests come from the same user.

Imagine going to a shop where the cashier doesn't remember you from one second to the next. You'd have to reintroduce yourself every time!

**Sessions** solve this problem by giving each user a unique ID and storing data on the server associated with that ID.

slides.title: How Sessions Work
slides.content:
# Session Mechanism

1. When a user visits your site for the first time, PHP creates a unique session ID (like a random string) and sends it to the browser, usually via a cookie.
2. The browser sends that session ID back with every subsequent request.
3. PHP uses that ID to load the session data from the server (usually files) into the `$_SESSION` superglobal.

This way, you can store user-specific information like login status, shopping cart items, etc., across page loads.

quizData.question: Why are sessions needed?
quizData.explanation: Sessions add state to the stateless HTTP protocol, allowing the server to remember users across requests.
